# Code Review — 2026-06-22 (symaira-corekit)

Scope: shared Go infra library (`exitcodes`, `logkit`, `envutil`, `fsutil`, `configkit`, `mcpserver`, `sqlitekit`, `updatecheck`). Evidence: package sources, `go.mod`, `Makefile`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, README. The library is well-hardened overall (constant-time compares are in the Pro repo; here: bounded reads in `mcpserver`/`updatecheck`, TLS 1.3 pinning + host-locked redirects, symlink-aware `SafeMkdirAll`). The items below are the highest-impact remaining levers.

- [ ] **[Security] SQLite pragmas are applied per-connection and silently lost on pooled connections**
  - **Status quo:** `Open` runs `PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON` and `PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000` via three separate `db.Exec` calls on the `*sql.DB` (`sqlitekit/sqlitekit.go:29-42`). `database/sql` runs each on an arbitrary pooled connection and returns it; `foreign_keys` and `busy_timeout` are **per-connection** settings that reset to SQLite defaults (FK **off**, timeout 0) on every other connection the pool opens. So FK enforcement — a documented safe default — is silently absent on most queries, and writers can hit immediate `SQLITE_BUSY` instead of waiting. (`journal_mode=WAL` is persisted in the file header, so only that one survives.)
  - **Proposed solution:** Set pragmas so they apply to *every* new connection. With `modernc.org/sqlite` use DSN pragmas: `sql.Open("sqlite", "file:"+path+"?_pragma=busy_timeout(5000)&_pragma=foreign_keys(1)&_pragma=journal_mode(WAL)")`. Alternatively register a connect hook / use `sql.OpenDB` with a custom connector, or `db.SetMaxOpenConns(1)` for single-writer local DBs. Add a test that opens N>1 connections concurrently and asserts `PRAGMA foreign_keys` returns 1.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / high impact (data-integrity correctness)

- [ ] **[Security] CI installs `govulncheck@latest` (unpinned), inconsistent with otherwise pinned toolchain**
  - **Status quo:** `.github/workflows/ci.yml:63` runs `go install golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest` while Go is pinned to `1.26.4` and actions are tag-pinned (`actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-go@v5`, lines 20/22/41/43/56/58). A `@latest` install means a compromised or breaking upstream release runs in CI unreviewed — a supply-chain/reproducibility gap, and a known CI trap for this ecosystem.
  - **Proposed solution:** Pin govulncheck to a known version (e.g. `govulncheck@v1.1.4`) or the `golang/govulncheck-action` at a fixed ref; for stronger hardening, SHA-pin the third-party/`actions/*` uses. Keep one canonical version string so it tracks with the Go bump.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[Security] `ValidatePath` permits tab/newline despite its documented "rejects control characters" contract**
  - **Status quo:** The doc says it rejects "Control characters (< 0x20)", but the loop explicitly carves out `\t` and `\n` (`fsutil/pathutil.go:34-38`). A validated path may therefore contain newlines/tabs, which can spoof or forge later log/CLI output (a path printed into structured logs or a terminal) and surprise consumers who trust the contract.
  - **Proposed solution:** Reject all control characters `< 0x20` (drop the `\t`/`\n` exception) and update the doc, or — if embedded tabs/newlines are genuinely required — document the exception explicitly and note that callers must escape such paths before display.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[UX/DX] configkit silently drops TOML array/slice (and map) config values**
  - **Status quo:** `setFromInterface` (`configkit/configkit.go:312-360`) handles only string/int/uint/float/bool; a TOML array applied to a `[]string`/`[]int` field hits no case and returns `nil` — silently dropped. The env path explicitly skips slices/maps (`configkit/configkit.go:245-246`). Consumers (`symfetch` allow-lists, `symseek` ignore globs, etc.) that put list config in TOML get no value and no error.
  - **Proposed solution:** Add slice handling to `setFromInterface` (iterate `[]interface{}`, convert each element via the existing scalar path). At minimum, return a clear error for unsupported field kinds instead of silently succeeding, matching the "convert-or-error" promise in the file's own comments (lines 158-160).
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact

- [ ] **[Architecture] Two parallel reflection walkers (TOML vs env) have already diverged**
  - **Status quo:** Config is applied twice through near-duplicate field-walking + conversion code: `applyMapToStruct`/`setFromInterface` for TOML and `applyEnvToFields`/`setFieldFromString` for env (`configkit/configkit.go:161-408`). They already differ in behavior — duration parsing, slice support, zero-value semantics, and error-on-bad-type all handled inconsistently between the two — which is exactly the slice gap above. Every new field type must be added in two places or silently misbehaves in one.
  - **Proposed solution:** Funnel both paths through one converter keyed on `reflect.Kind` (+ a `time.Duration` special case), with the source layer only responsible for producing a normalized value. One code path → no drift, and the slice/map gaps close once.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Medium effort / medium impact

> Performance: no standalone high-impact lever found — these are small, allocation-light modules with no hot loops. The only measurable startup cost (three serial SQLite PRAGMA round-trips in `Open`) disappears for free once the pragmas move into the DSN/connector in the Security item above.

## Conclusion
First recommended quick win: **fix the SQLite pragma application in `sqlitekit.Open`** — it is a low-effort change that restores the `foreign_keys=ON` data-integrity guarantee (and busy-timeout behavior) across the whole connection pool, and incidentally removes the startup round-trips.
